title: The neuroscience of why grounding techniques work for some and not others

htmlContent:
The promise that didn't land

I remember the first time someone told me to "just breathe." I was in a therapist's office, two years into recovery from what I now understand was complex trauma. My nervous system was a live wire, humming with a voltage that felt like it could scorch through skin at any moment. And this well-meaning professional looked at me and said, "Have you tried grounding? Just name five things you can see."

I wanted to scream. Actually, I wanted to throw something. But I sat there, obedient, and tried to name five things. Chair. Window. Plant. Lamp. Carpet. Nothing changed. My heart was still pounding. My chest still felt like a cage someone had welded shut. The dissociation didn't lift. The panic didn't soften.

Here's the thing: I thought I was broken. I thought grounding techniques were supposed to work, and if they didn't work for me, it meant I was doing it wrong. Or worse, that I was too damaged to be helped.

It took years to understand the truth. Grounding techniques aren't a failure of will or a sign of being "too far gone." They're a mismatch between the tool and the wiring. And the science behind why some people can ground and others can't is actually pretty straightforward once you understand the nervous system.

The vagus nerve and the window of tolerance

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system actually works. He found that we have three primary states: ventral vagal (safe and social, where grounding can actually land), sympathetic (fight or flight, where grounding gets harder), and dorsal vagal (shutdown or collapse, where grounding often fails entirely).

Here's what most people don't tell you: grounding techniques require a certain baseline of nervous system regulation to work. Think about it. If you're in ventral vagal, mildly anxious but still connected to your body, naming five things can pull you back into presence. Your system has enough capacity to follow the instruction.

But if you're in dorsal vagal shutdown, your body has pressed the emergency brake. Your brain stem has determined that survival requires disconnecting from sensation. And someone telling you to feel your feet on the floor? That's asking a system that has turned off its sensory input to suddenly turn it back on. It's like asking a computer that's crashed to run a program. It can't. Not yet.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "window of tolerance," explained this better than anyone. Your window of tolerance is the zone where you can process experience without becoming overwhelmed or shut down. Trauma narrows this window. When you're outside of it, either hyperaroused (panic, rage, hypervigilance) or hypoaroused (numb, collapsed, dissociated), your brain literally cannot integrate sensory information the same way.

I've had clients tell me they feel worse after grounding. Their heart races more. Their dissociation deepens. They feel like they're suffocating. And I tell them: that's not failure. That's information. Your nervous system is saying, "I can't do that right now. Try something else."

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through exactly why some interventions work and others don't, and he's honest about the complexity. It's not a quick fix. It's a map.

Why your nervous system might reject the 5-4-3-2-1 technique

Let's get specific. The 5-4-3-2-1 technique asks you to name five things you see, four you can touch, three you hear, two you smell, and one you taste. It's everywhere. It's in every trauma workbook, every crisis hotline script, every well-meaning Instagram post.

And for some people, it works beautifully. For others, it's a disaster.

Here's why. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talked about the importance of "pendulation" moving between activation and settling. The 5-4-3-2-1 technique assumes you can access your senses without becoming overwhelmed. But if your nervous system is stuck in a trauma response, your sensory processing is compromised.

Think about what happens in a real threat. Your brain narrows focus. Peripheral vision drops. Hearing becomes selective. Touch becomes either hypervigilant or numb. This is adaptive. It helps you survive. But it also means that asking someone in a trauma state to "notice what you hear" might flood them with overwhelming sensory input they've been working hard to filter out.

I've worked with survivors of sexual assault who cannot tolerate feeling their own skin. The sensation of touch triggers flashbacks. Telling them to "notice the texture of your shirt" isn't grounding. It's a trigger. Stay with me here. The technique isn't bad. The mismatch between the technique and the nervous system state is the problem.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine writes about how the body holds trauma and what actually helps release it. It's not about forcing techniques. It's about listening to what the body needs.

The dorsal vagal problem no one talks about

Here's where it gets really specific. When your nervous system drops into dorsal vagal shutdown, your body enters a conservation state. Metabolism slows. Heart rate drops. Blood pressure decreases. You feel heavy, slow, disconnected. This is the freeze or collapse response.

And grounding techniques that ask you to "feel your body" or "notice your breath" can actually make this worse. Why? Because your body is already telling you to disconnect. The dorsal vagal state is a survival strategy. It's your system's way of saying, "We can't fight. We can't flee. Let's play dead until the danger passes."

Asking someone in this state to "come back to their body" is asking them to override a survival response. That's not impossible, but it requires a different approach. You can't just jump from collapse to calm. You have to move through activation first. You have to let the system discharge the trapped energy that's keeping it stuck.

Janina Fisher, who integrates parts work with trauma treatment, talks about this beautifully. She says that different parts of us respond to different interventions. The part that's in shutdown might need gentle movement, not stillness. The part that's hypervigilant might need containment, not expansion. The part that's dissociated might need orienting, not grounding.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). It's a classic for a reason. Levine walks through how animals in the wild complete their stress cycles, and what that means for humans who never got to finish their trauma responses.

What actually works when grounding fails

So if grounding doesn't work for you, what do you do? The answer is not "try harder." The answer is to match the intervention to your nervous system state.

Here's a rough guide based on what I've learned from Deb Dana's work on polyvagal theory in practice:

If you're in fight or flight (sympathetic activation): grounding might help, but only if you do it with movement. Try walking slowly while naming what you see. Or pressing your hands against a wall and pushing gently. The key is to give the activation somewhere to go. Stillness can feel like suffocation when your system wants to run.

If you're in shutdown (dorsal activation): grounding often fails. Try orienting instead. Slowly turn your head to look around the room. Notice what catches your eye. Let your gaze wander without trying to focus. This activates the ventral vagal system without demanding sensory integration that isn't available.

If you're in ventral vagal but distressed: grounding can work beautifully. This is the sweet spot. Your system is regulated enough to follow the instruction. Name those five things. Feel your feet. It'll probably help.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports GABA function and helps calm the nervous system. It's not a cure, but it can make the window of tolerance a little wider. I take it myself on days when my system feels raw.

Honestly, the most important thing I've learned is this: stop fighting your nervous system. If a technique doesn't work, put it down. Try something else. The goal isn't to force yourself into calm. The goal is to find what your particular wiring needs in this particular moment.

Some days that's a cold shower. Some days it's lying on the floor with a weighted blanket. Some days it's calling a friend. Some days it's just surviving until bedtime. All of it counts. All of it is valid.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something soften? Did something tense up? That's the data you need. Your nervous system is talking to you constantly. The question is whether you're ready to listen.
